metadata.title: Overview of comments received on 'Guideline on clinical evaluation of medicinal products used in weight control' (EMA/CHMP/311805/2014)
metadata.note: This is a public consultation 'overview of comments received' document (EMA/414592/2016, CHMP, 23 June 2016). It is NOT the primary guideline. Rules extracted exclusively from the 'Outcome' column, which records official CHMP responses/positions to stakeholder comments on draft guideline EMA/CHMP/311805/2014. Stakeholder comments themselves have not been extracted as rules. The document covers 4 stakeholders (EASD, EASO, EFPIA, Prescrire) across Section 1 (General comments) and Section 2 (Specific comments on text), totalling 27 pages.

rules.rule_text: Weight loss is considered the most relevant primary efficacy endpoint from a regulatory point of view for medicinal products used in weight control. Improvements in waist circumference and/or body composition are only recommended as key secondary efficacy endpoints, as their clinical relevance is less well established.
rules.verbatim_quote: Weight loss is considered as the most relevant endpoint from a regulatory point of view as it is associated with clinically relevant beneficial effects. The clinical relevance of the magnitude of improvements in waist circumference and/or body composition is still less well established. These outcomes are therefore only recommended as key secondary efficacy endpoints in the current guideline.
rules.exceptions: Where validated equipment is available, body composition measurements may be added to the standard portfolio of measurements as secondary endpoints.

rules.rule_text: A direct demonstration of reduction in morbidity and/or mortality is NOT an absolute requirement for approval of a weight-reducing agent. Surrogate markers of efficacy — including CV risk factors and body weight — are considered reliable. A mandatory mortality/morbidity outcome study prior to marketing authorisation is not required, as such a study would be of considerable length and would delay patient access.
rules.verbatim_quote: A direct demonstration of a reduction of mortality/morbidity is therefore not considered as an absolute requirement for approval of a weight reducing agent. Indeed such a study would be of considerable length and would delay access of the medicines to patients which is not considered as adequate considering that the surrogate markers of efficacy (i.e. CV risk factors including loss of body weight) are considered as reliable.
rules.exceptions: Weight loss must be associated with clinically relevant beneficial effects. Safety profile must be carefully evaluated to ensure benefits outweigh risks. CV risk factors including body weight must confirm adequate benefit-risk balance.
cross_references: CHMP Reflection paper on assessment of cardiovascular safety profile of medicinal products

rules.rule_text: The safety profile of any drug intended for weight management must be carefully evaluated to ensure that the benefits indeed outweigh any risks. The safety section of the guideline has been reinforced compared to the previous version (2007), with particular attention to requirements for the evaluation and quantification of the cardiovascular risk at the time of licensing.
rules.verbatim_quote: Partly accepted. There is no reason to believe that weight loss as a result of drug therapy would not have a similar beneficial effect on morbidity and mortality compared to weight loss due to life style interventions. However, the safety profile of the drug must be carefully evaluated to ensure that the benefits indeed outweigh any risks. The safety section of the guideline has been reinforced compared to the previous version, and with regard to the requirements for the evaluation and quantification of the cardiovascular risk at the time of licensing the GL further refers to the CHMP's 'Reflection paper on assessment of cardiovascular safety profile of medicinal products'.
cross_references: CHMP Reflection paper on assessment of cardiovascular safety profile of medicinal products

rules.rule_text: Pharmacological options for weight control are not recommended until at least one trial of an appropriate weight-reducing diet has proved insufficient (i.e. inadequate initial weight loss was achieved, or the individual could not maintain an initial weight loss despite continuing dietary advice). Pharmacological options are only considered as an adjunct to dietary measures and physical exercise. Eligible patients are those for whom at least one trial of an appropriate weight-reducing diet has proven to be insufficient.
rules.verbatim_quote: Pharmacological options are not recommended until at least one trial of an appropriate weight-reducing diet has proved insufficient, i.e. inadequate initial weight loss was achieved or the individual, despite continuing dietary advice, could not maintain an initial weight loss. Pharmacological options are only considered as an adjunct to dietary measures and physical exercise. [...] Eligible patients are those for whom at least one trial of an appropriate weight-reducing diet has proven to be insufficient.
rules.exceptions: There is no absolute requirement for a run-in period in trial design; however, the above patient eligibility criterion applies at trial entry.

rules.rule_text: A minimum trial duration of 12 months is considered adequate to assess the effect on weight management. For studies aiming to document effects on some weight-related outcomes (e.g. delay in onset or prevention of type 2 diabetes), longer study durations may be needed. Longer durations may also be required for safety reasons, and can be requested either pre- or post-approval.
rules.verbatim_quote: Partly accepted. To assess effect on weight management, 12 months duration is considered as adequate. However, the guideline also states that 'To document the effect on some weight related outcomes (e.g. delay in onset/prevention of type 2 diabetes), longer study durations may be needed'. Also for safety reasons, longer durations may be requested pre or post approval.
rules.exceptions: Longer durations required when making claims about weight-related outcomes beyond body weight reduction (e.g. prevention of type 2 diabetes) or when safety signals require extended follow-up.

rules.rule_text: Pivotal studies for weight management drugs must be placebo-controlled. As new weight management drugs become available in the EU, active-controlled trial designs may also be relevant in addition to placebo-controlled trials.
rules.verbatim_quote: Partly accepted. Studies should be placebo controlled, but products approved in the EU may also be included in the studies; 'as new weight management drugs will become available in the EU, it is recognized that active-controlled trial designs may be relevant in addition to placebo-controlled trials'.
rules.exceptions: Active-controlled arms are acceptable in addition to (not instead of) placebo control when approved weight management drugs are available in the EU.

rules.rule_text: All reported adverse events must be assessed irrespective of the mechanism of action of the investigational product. The adverse reactions specifically mentioned in the guideline (including neuropsychiatric effects, cardiovascular effects, and a section on abuse potential) are those requiring extra attention from sponsors. Cardiovascular safety evaluation must follow the CHMP's 'Reflection paper on assessment of cardiovascular safety profile of medicinal products'. Drug interactions must be assessed in line with relevant PK guidelines.
rules.verbatim_quote: Partly accepted. As for all medicinal products, all reported adverse events will be assessed irrespective of mechanism of action. The adverse reactions specifically mentioned in the guideline (including a section on abuse potential) are those that require extra attention from sponsors. With respect to CV safety, references are made to the CHMP's 'Reflection paper on assessment of cardiovascular safety profile of medicinal products'. With respect to interactions, reference is made to relevant PK guidelines.
cross_references: CHMP Reflection paper on assessment of cardiovascular safety profile of medicinal products

rules.rule_text: A drug with an adverse, unfavourable effect on any cardiovascular risk factor triggers an obligation to perform a cardiovascular outcome study, independent of the results of a meta-analysis of cardiovascular endpoint observed in the study programme. The evaluation and quantification of CV safety for drugs intended for weight management is addressed in the CHMP's 'Reflection paper on assessment of cardiovascular safety profile of medicinal products'.
rules.source_section: Section 2 – Specific comments on text, Lines 312–314
rules.verbatim_quote: Accepted. This issue is covered in more detail in the 'Reflection paper on assessment of cardiovascular safety profile of medicinal products'.
rules.exceptions: Drugs with no adverse effects on CV risk factors, and those with small beneficial effects on risk factors, are assessed on actual effects rather than a blanket requirement. The level of obligation scales with the nature of the identified CV risk signal.
cross_references: CHMP Reflection paper on assessment of cardiovascular safety profile of medicinal products

rules.rule_text: For recently approved weight management products, the approved indication wording is: '[Product] is indicated, as an adjunct to a reduced-calorie diet and increased physical activity, for the management of weight in adult patients.' The guideline focuses on weight management and not maintenance of effect; maintenance of effect is not part of the indication statement.
rules.source_section: Section 2 – Specific comments on text, Line 137
rules.verbatim_quote: Not Accepted. For recently approved products, the wording of the indication has been: X is indicated, as an adjunct to a reduced-calorie diet and increased physical activity, for the management of weight in adult patients, i.e. no mentioning of maintenance of effect. Therefore, the guidance will focus on management and not maintenance.
rules.exceptions: Weight maintenance studies may be conducted but are not required for the core indication label. Separate claims may require separate evidence.

rules.rule_text: The EU efficacy threshold for weight loss in pivotal trials is 5–10% of baseline body weight, which is not considered dissimilar from the FDA's 5% threshold. EU and FDA requirements are regarded as broadly aligned. Full harmonisation of endpoints is not adopted, but the requirements are not considered substantially different.
rules.source_section: Section 2 – Specific comments on text, Lines 140–155
rules.verbatim_quote: Not accepted. The requirements are not that dissimilar; EU 5-10 % vs FDA 5%.
rules.exceptions: Two complementary primary efficacy analysis approaches are described in the guideline: (1) mean percentage weight loss vs baseline compared to placebo, and (2) proportion of responders achieving ≥10% weight loss (primary) and ≥5% weight loss (secondary) at 12 months.
cross_references: FDA guidance on developing products for weight management (Feb 2007)

rules.rule_text: When there are any indications of neuropsychiatric safety issues from mechanistic, non-clinical, early clinical or marketed data, prospective assessment of psychiatric, neurostimulant or cognitive adverse events must be included in the clinical development programme, using the best tools currently available. The guideline text has been amended to reflect this broader trigger.
rules.source_section: Section 2 – Specific comments on text, Lines 275–282
rules.verbatim_quote: Partly accepted. Text amended in line with the suggested text, but more specific guidance concerning the preferred scoring tools/scales to assess neuropsychiatric side effects is considered to be outside the scope of this guideline.
rules.exceptions: The specific preferred scoring tools or scales to assess neuropsychiatric side effects are outside the scope of this guideline; sponsors should use the best tools currently available.

rules.rule_text: If there are any indications from mechanistic, non-clinical, early clinical or marketed data, then prospective assessment of abuse potential, dependence, and/or withdrawal effects must be included in the clinical programme, using the best tools currently available.
rules.source_section: Section 2 – Specific comments on text, Line 286
rules.verbatim_quote: Accepted. [Proposed text accepted:] 'If there are any indications from mechanistic, non-clinical, early clinical or marketed data, then prospective assessment of abuse potential, dependence, and/or withdrawal effects should be included with the best tools currently available.'
rules.exceptions: Requirement is triggered by indications from any of the following data sources: mechanistic, non-clinical, early clinical, or marketed data. Not required as a blanket universal assessment in the absence of any such signals.

rules.rule_text: The evaluation of pulmonary arterial hypertension (PAH) and valvulopathy is considered an integral part of the cardiovascular safety evaluation for weight management drugs and must be addressed. This evaluation is briefly discussed in section 7.4 of the guideline; a separate dedicated section for PAH is not required as the topic is already covered within the CV safety framework.
rules.source_section: Section 2 – Specific comments on text, Lines 345–346
rules.verbatim_quote: Accepted. The evaluation of PAH and valvulopathy is considered to be an integral part of the CV safety evaluation and is briefly discussed in section 7.4. Therefore propose to delete this section.
rules.exceptions: Non-clinical studies to discharge PAH risk should be based on the mechanism of action and any known class effects of the drug.
cross_references: CHMP Reflection paper on assessment of cardiovascular safety profile of medicinal products
